posts.name: J a y H a c k
posts.cooked: The CT vs. experts thread got me thinking. What are your best blind tasting “gets.” How about your worst? There have to be some fun stories out there. I’ll leave my best for later, but my worst was saying that the 2007 Saxum James Berry was a thin, insipid, overly acidic wine that had no flavor and did nothing for me.
posts.display_username: J a y H a c k

posts.name: Ben Sherwin
posts.cooked:
My best was nailing an '88 Lynch Bages and an '06 Keller double blind on the same night. Chris Kravitz correctly noted I wouldn’t be insufferable after that.

The same Mr. Kravitz nailed a stunning '81 Unico double blind at a BDX dinner a few years ago. I think I was somewhere in Italy, but completely guessing, only thinking it wasn’t Bordeaux (although I wasn’t certain). That was a staggering work of blind tasting genius. I have to note it every time I talk about the subject.

My worst was when Ben Goldberg served me '82 Gruaud Larose, which I described as how BDX should taste after I had repeated trashed GL. Doh! Blind tasting keeps you humble.

posts.name: Glenn Bienstock
posts.cooked: I nailed 2001 Charvin double blind.

posts.name: Bryan Carr
posts.cooked: Don’t have a ton of experience tasting blind but I went to dinner a while back and had a Dolcetto that I absolutely hated. Fast forward a month or so and I went with my fiancee to pick up some wines at Full Pull, where they always pour you something blind when you pick up. Same damn wine and I knew right away. My fiancee didn’t even remember the wine from dinner and was astonished. My palate isn’t very sophisticated, just spiteful.

posts.name: Brig Campbell
posts.cooked: In a blind flight of Pinot noir and was convinced the entire flight was Syrah. Oops.

posts.name: Bryan Carr
posts.cooked:
brigcampbell:

In a blind flight of Pinot noir and was convinced the entire flight was Syrah. Oops.

That reminds me of when Lauren and I were at the Girl and the Fig in August. We ordered glasses of Syrah and Grenache from local producers we had never tried and when the server placed them down we were surprised at how much we loved the Grenache and how bored we were with the Syrah. We talked about it back and forth, how we’d never had such a meaty peppery Grenache, and how much it reminded us of the types of Northern Rhone and Rocks Syrah we usually love. After dinner we called all over and found the Grenache at retail ($18 or something quite cheap), walked across town to buy a bottle (thinking we might buy a whole case) and went back to our hotel room triumphantly, bottle in hand. We cracked it open and lo and behold it was the “Syrah” from dinner we didn’t like. The server had just mixed them up on his tray on the way over. In hindsight, duh the meaty peppery delicious thing we both loved was obviously a Syrah, I think the color is what threw him and us off too, as the Grenache was rather purple and the Syrah was a fairly red.

posts.name: Ed Gonzales
posts.cooked:
several years ago buzz, bc little, mike sai and i had a blind tasting at buzz’s palatial estate. we had a roumier in the mix among other burgs and higher end pinot…we all thought (i did anyway) that we had the roumier pegged and that it was easily the wine of the flight. the reveal had us floored - the wine ended up being an oregon pinot that mike lifted for $4? at a local Grocery Outlet.

i’m not even going to tell you about the time i thought a 94 gallo cab was blue chip left bank behemoth.

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked:
Best ever was nailing the 1985 Bartolo Mascarello Barolo completely blind. Wine was brought to the table in a decanter. Took about 10 minutes of figuring to get wine type, producer and vintage.

There have been a few other close calls, but that was the best.

Worst was calling a Willi Schaefer Kabinett a “second rate Alsace Pinot Gris.”

posts.name: Bryan Carr
posts.cooked:
At Peter H’s place earlier this year the whole table tag-teamed Cayuse God Only Knows blind, each person got a piece and it snowballed into a correct guess on my part: Jay (“is this a Rhone Ranger?”), Amelie (“this has got to be Grenache”), Lauren (“the nose screams ‘The Rocks’”) and me (I basically just named the first Rocks Grenache I could think of thereby stealing all the credit).

I came back from the bathroom at a tasting a while back with my glass refilled with something red and looked down at the closest bottle. “Wow this definitely has that great dusty quality I like in Merlot, great typicity…” cue Lauren “uh, honey, you’re drinking the Malbec…” open mouth insert foot take off ascot

posts.name: Mike Evans
posts.cooked:
Best was at a friend’s annual blind tasting. The format is that you get 1 point each for vintage, country, region, variety, and specific wine. I was 3 points behind going into the last wine. It puzzled me for a few minutes, then it clicked, while the rest of the group was completely flummoxed, with all sorts of wild guesses being made. When I asked the host if the wine had a Sasquatch on the label, everyone else thought I was making a joke and joining in the fun (one wag had thrown out “South Carolina carignan” as his guess), but the host looked like someone just walked over his grave. I correctly identified the wine as the 2014 Dirty and Rowdy Familiar Mourvèdre for 5 points and the win. I’ve nailed wines blind before, but getting to ask about the Sasquatch put this one solidly at the top of the list.

My worst blind tasting story was at the same event a few years before, where I scored about 9 points from a possible 50, with less than 1/2 the points of the next lowest contestant, and I think most of the points I had were dumb luck.

I learned a long time ago to profess my guesses strongly and with confidence. Do so, and people will remember the successes for years, while the memories of even the most spectacular misses tend to fade quickly.

Funny, mine is the opposite. I called a flight of Syrah a flight of Pinot Noir. There have been a couple of good times too, but I think the bad ones are more important to remember.

posts.name: Victor Hong
posts.cooked: Recently, I nailed one wine as a bottle of cr@p, double blind.

posts.name: Humberto Dorta
posts.cooked:
My favorite ever was when my buddy brought a bottle covered and poured it blind. I correctly, loudly and with much swirling, sniffing and pomp, pronouced it 2005 Red Truck. He was dumb founded. I never explained to him that I had never had the wine but it was in a brown paper bag, which is what the PLCB bags in and that is the only store option in PA, and I knew the local one like the back of my hand, I knew he would go to the cab and red blend section and pick out the most expensive bottle there. It was to be a wine night where we typically would drink higher end stuff but he forgot to bring bottle from home At the time, sadly, the most expensive red in the store was red truck. Nowadays a buddy has taken over the store and thankfully has packed it full of goodies

The worst was when educated guess finished second of 28 cabs in a group of 16 drinkers. It was only out voted by Maybach but also present were harlan, kapcsandy, colgin, pride reserve, dominus, schrader, etc

posts.name: Ian Sutton
posts.cooked:
Good blind guess: Guessing Muscadet when I’d only ever had one before and that over a decade before tasting the blind wine.

Bad blind guess: At a wine society tasting walkround, guessing Musar Pere et Fils as a Rhone wine. A shame we’d drunk that very same wine with our tasting group earlier that month, indeed considering it the best wine that night. Such a short memory.

posts.name: Brig Campbell
posts.cooked:
I thought a Musar Blanc was a rosé


  • 2001 Chateau Musar Blanc - Lebanon, Bekaa Valley (2/27/2014).

G5 - Variety is the Spice of Life (Juliette): Thought it was rosé until the label was displayed, ah! This is on my bucket list of wines because of the uniqueness. Push the light peach color on the spectrum driven by the oxidation, it is a white wine. I really struggled to decode the aromas, I was stumped. Palate has a very strong salinity, most forward I’ve tasted, right from the attack. There is unripe nectarine and lemon flavors. Finish showed a hint of anise. Really cool experience.

Posted from CellarTracker

posts.name: Ken Vastola
posts.cooked: I once solved a puzzle on Wheel of Fortune without seeing any letters.

posts.name: Victor Hong
posts.cooked: I can name that song in one note.

posts.name: Cole Kendall
posts.cooked: My all time favorite blind event was when I was pouring and had to keep a straight face as a guy who posts a lot of notes here on high end wine called “Chave” (tasting was single, not double blind, so he knew there was a Chave in the line-up) on a bottle of Petite Sirah.

posts.name: Craig Gleason
posts.cooked: We had a single-blind tasting at Rhys with a flight of 2010. 2012 and 2013 Alpine and Horseshoe Chardonnays. I listened to Kevin’s description of the relative characteristics of the two vineyards and then I picked each pair exactly wrong.

posts.name: J a y H a c k
posts.cooked: There was the time that someone I know who is on this BB and is a confirmed AFWE proclaimed that he really liked a particular wine and said it was outstanding. Upon reveal, it was the 2002 Termanthia. We all agreed that we would not rat him out, so his identity must remain a secret.

Worse: Confusing a cabernet with a zinfandel in the above tasting. Still not sure how that occurred.

Ben

posts.name: David Cohen
posts.cooked:
Was on a cruise doing a tasting and the last wine was a 97 opus (not blind). They were trying to fancy so wait staff who were pouring had white gloves on and they had napkins tied around the bottles to catch drippings. Anyhow long story short in my thick obnoxious NY accent I tell the other couple we were doing tasting with this is NOT a 97 opus but rather a 96 opus or possible a 97 opus which was a little off. Being a loud guy the wine steward hears and assures us its a 97. He called waitress over and untied the napkin to reveal the 96 vintage. Next day when we returned to our cabin there was a bottle of 96 and 97 waiting for us.
I had done a mini vertical of opus (95-99) a few months before the cruise.
I have called 2 or 3 wines double blind in my blind tastering group over the past few years but the cruise story has always stuck out.

2001 Muller Catoir Haardter Mandelring Scheurebe Spatlese
2009 Clos Roche Blanche Cot

Of course in the same flight as the latter wine I guessed Cornas for a Bordeaux.

posts.name: Robert Dentice
posts.cooked:
My favorite blind tasting story is that I poured a three wine flight of the same wine. However one wine was open for 3 days, one 24 hours and the last pop and pour.

The winemaker was present plus a table of additional winemakers and somms. All had radically different guesses.

posts.name: Anton Dotson
posts.cooked:
Best:

I tasted a pasta dish at a friend’s house and said, “This pasta sauce was made with both an over the hill 1962 BV cab and an overly ‘bretted’ 1983 Quail Ridge cab.”

The hostess was dumbfounded, and went to the recycling to check, and I was right. The crowd went wild.

(I didn’t mention that her husband and I had tried them the previous weekend and I knew he put them into his ‘cooking queue.’ He graciously kept his yap shut.)

Worst:

I snuck a 2 Buck Chuck and Kendall Jackson chard to a new wine tasting group’s blind “High End Chardonnay” meeting as ringers…and they took first and second. The group didn’t think it was so funny and it was my first and last experience with the group. (This was when I was living in Las Vegas, so what did I expect?) It also killed my chance to be invited to join the Chaîne des Rôtisseurs.

posts.name: andy velebil
posts.cooked:
Mine happened in the same day, with the same producer. Was visiting a prominent Douro (Portugal) wine and Port producer for the day (lunch and dinner). I should add this is one of my favorite Douro wine producers. During lunch he served a number of his wines blind (7 bottles IIRC). Both dry wine and Port. I guessed every single bottle correctly except for one. That included vintage, grape varietal (some were single grape varietal wines, some blends), and what wine it was. The producer was stunned as he had put in some older wines not seen or tasted very often. He asked if I had sneaked off into the kitchen before to look (I had not).

Forward to dinner. Same blind format, same number of wines. Except this time I only got one wine correct and was wrong about all the others. We all had a good laugh over that.

posts.name: Anton Dotson
posts.cooked:

I blame palate fatigue when that happens.

posts.name: Joe Webb
posts.cooked:
I have had 2 double blind guesses on wines where they were assumed no one quess, a '82 Hanzell cab and a Flowers Pinot Meunier.

I once put a magnum of Korbel in a flight of mags of bubbles. It won the tasting with somms in training, winemakers, and wine biz folks. The look on thier faces when I revealed it was priceless. I am known as a bubble head so no one thought i would do such a thing, I also brought roederer l’ermitage to make up for the korbel.

posts.name: Ken Vastola
posts.cooked:
Anton D:

I snuck a 2 Buck Chuck and Kendall Jackson chard to a new wine tasting group’s blind “High End Chardonnay” meeting as ringers…and they took first and second. The group didn’t think it was so funny and it was my first and last experience with the group. (This was when I was living in Las Vegas, so what did I expect?) It also killed my chance to be invited to join the Chaîne des Rôtisseurs.

Probably a crappy group. I stuck a 2 Buck Chuck 2001 Cab into a 2001 Cab blind tasting with my group up here. People were commenting on every wine, but when they got to this one, they just tasted it and moved on. It had no flaws, but it had nothing of interest. They just moved on like it didn’t exist.

posts.name: Rajiv Ayyangar
posts.cooked:
My worst is probably that I blinded a Beaujolais as a Nebbiolo, then a week later blinded the same bottle as a Chianti Classico (my friends are sometimes mean).

My best overall performance is probably this flight I did with a friend studying for his Advanced Somm exam:

Called: 2011 Pinot Grigio
Actual: 2010 Chapoutier Crozes-Hermitage Petite Ruche
(This is the only wine I totally bombed - I chalk it up to lack of calibration on structure).

Called: 2014 Muscadet
Actual: 2013 Claude Branger Muscadet

Called: 2012 Anderson Valley Pinot
Actual: 2013 Copain Pinot Les Voisins

Called: 2006 Right Bank Bordeaux
Actual: 2006 Chateau Canon

Called: White burgundy, recent vintage
Actual: 2014 JA Ferret Pouilly-Fuisse

Called: 2013 Bordeaux Blanc
Actual: 2013 Mondavi Fume Blanc Reserve, To Kalon Vineyard
(I think this is a fair confusion - the Mondavi was fairly lean and crisp).

Called: 2012 Red Burgundy, Premier Cru quality
Actual: 2012 Soter Pinot, North Valley Reserve
(Here I was a bit off on the fruit ripeness)

Called: New Zealand Pinot, recent vintage
Actual: 2012 Alexana Pinot Noir, Dundee Hills, Willamette Valley

posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked:
told this story in a similar thread but worth retelling. Wife has a great palate but wants nothing to do with talking about wine–just give her a good glass. We were with another couple at Addison where I’d always taste the somms on our wines and they’d blind me on one of theirs, usually a Burg.

This time they brought over a glass, I swirled and smelled, sipped and gargled, puzzled over it. Wife, deep in conversation with her friend, grabs the glass and casually takes a sip, and says “89 Lynch Bages,” then goes right back to talking. I had to do CPR on the somm–wife was right.

posts.name: J a y H a c k
posts.cooked:
Robert Dentice:

My favorite blind tasting story is that I poured a three wine flight of the same wine. However one wine was open for 3 days, one 24 hours and the last pop and pour.

The winemaker was present plus a table of additional winemakers and somms. All had radically different guesses.

I did a variation of that with a double magnum of La Chapelle so people could not even claim bottle variation. One person noticed a similarity and said same producer, different vintages, other did not have a clue.
posts.display_username: J a y H a c k

posts.name: Mikhail Lipyanskiy
posts.cooked:
Best - 2005 ROSSO CÀ DEL MERLO GIUSEPPE QUINTARELLI
double blind - a friend brought a bottle wrapped in foil to dinner. Dinner had no other veneto wines.

my guess was 05 Quitarelli Valpolicella… how? no idea. while i have been lucky enough to have Quintarelli wines before (perhaps 2-3 times) - never this one.

worst? i am sure there were a ton … the most recent was mistaking 13 nebbiolo langhe for a california pinot. i did think that the 07 chaupin was port but that may not be that far off

posts.name: Anton Dotson
posts.cooked:
alan weinberg:

told this story in a similar thread but worth retelling. Wife has a great palate but wants nothing to do with talking about wine–just give her a good glass. We were with another couple at Addison where I’d always taste the somms on our wines and they’d blind me on one of theirs, usually a Burg.

This time they brought over a glass, I swirled and smelled, sipped and gargled, puzzled over it. Wife, deep in conversation with her friend, grabs the glass and casually takes a sip, and says “89 Lynch Bages,” then goes right back to talking. I had to do CPR on the somm–wife was right.

I have a sister in law like that. She has the gustatory equivalent of a photographic memory.

I would’ve loved to see the look on the sommeliers face. Especially since she was so offhanded but precise about it.

posts.name: Rauno E N G el
posts.cooked: The most impressive I’ve seen was hosting a dinner many years ago, heavy with wine geekdom. One of the wives (frankly, she should never be considered the “plus 1”) started by correctly naming the four types of fish in the bouillabaisse. She then laughed and pronounced “Chablis, probably Clos” correctly when murmurings at the table were all around Puligny, then nailed “Spanish, very young, new age sort” on the last red ('93 l’Ermita - pretty much just released)

posts.name: Ken Vastola
posts.cooked: My group here had a Pomerol tasting not blind. At the end, I pulled out a bottle of Newton Merlot wrapped in plain brown paper. By coincidence, someone was talking about Petrus and how we didn’t have any at the tasting. I guess due to the power of suggestion, people thought my bottle might be Petrus, at least a very young Petrus. As it went around the room, it got to our leader. As he poured it, having not tasted it, he said, “This isn’t Petrus, it’s Newton Merlot.” I had to pick up my jaw from the floor.

posts.name: Ralph Earle
posts.cooked:
This is sort of blind: At my last Boston Wine Festival (1990), I was handed a Meritage. I correctly named the percentage of each of the five grapes. At the same event, after tasting a Ferrari-Carano Cabernet Sauvignon I asked the wine maker, “Do you tumble your grapes?” He was astounded, because at that time they did.

The worst stories are within the past five years: 0 for everything on mystery wines my wife brings home from Costco.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
I was wondering if I should tell this, but on reflection if I don’t mention the name of the winery, and it was thirty years ago, it’s too good a story not to tell.

In the late 1980s there used to be a wine weekend held at the Desmond in Albany. One of the highlights was an afternoon session for professionals, where everybody bought a bottle and served it blind. Knowing the owner was going to be there, I brought a bottle of his 1977 California Pinot Noir. The wine served before it had been a Rousseau so it didn’t show well in comparison, in fact was a little sweet and boring. Unfortunately the owner really hated it, saying why bother making wine like this, and ranting for a few minutes as to why wines like this should never be released. Incredibly embarrassing, and a good early lesson in learning what you can and can’t serve blind.

Single blind
8 or 10 years ago I went to a Chateau Latour dinner where 8 vintages were poured blind - 2 at a time. Our host had chosen someone different to talk about each pair of wines before the years were revealed.
I was sitting between to women, in the wine business, one from Burgundy and one from California.When we received the first pair, it was obvious these were very old examples of Latour. I Don’t think either of my table mates had ever had wines this old , so I decided to venture a guess and figured our host might choose a consistent interval between the wines-so I guessed 1909/1919. The first speaker gave what I call the wine babble speech about color,fruit,balance, etc, but never saying anything specific about these wines and never guessing the year.The host finally looked over and asked if he had a guess- he replied 20’s-30’s at which point he revealed them to be 1911 and 1919. I told my table mates not to expect me to get close the rest of the night. Then we get to the last pair ( didn’t get any of the next 4 ) and we haven’t had 59 or 61 and I figure we’ll see at least one of them. I taste the first wine and conclude it can only be the 61 ( based on the quality of the fruit, precision,purity, length and elegance and the fact that it wasn’t jammy like the 59 ) , but can’t figure out the second. the chosen speaker starts out by saying , “he’s had the 61 many times and this isn’t it.” He then proceeds to insert his foot fully down his throat for the next 5 minutes by saying its not the 61,but the 59 based on lots of different criteria. Finally the host puts him out of his misery by disclosing its the 61.

Double blind
I was in Paris a few years ago and staying at a hotel with a multiple star Michelin restaurant and had the opportunity ( didn’t eat in the restaurant, just met him as he poured some wines I had brought to drink with my son in the garden) meet the head somm, and I invited him to join us in drinking some wines.
After I opened 2 or 3 bottles , he asked if he could offer a bottle for us to drink. Of course I accepted and he then served this white blind. I smelled and tasted it and said it reminded me of 07 Raveneau Montee de Tonnerre ( of which we’d been drinking recently ) - floored , he then revealed the same wine.
Always a lot of fun when you can nail or get close in a blind tasting- although its usually few and far between. Cheers
btw I try to forget the disasters as quickly as possible

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Greatest recent triumph was identifying a 1996 Cotat Cuvée Speciale as such, which is quite an eclectic wine. But I have drunk quite a bit of Cotat over the years.

Without naming a certain prolific Master of Wine, I will relate that I once listened to him rhapsodizing about the merits of Les Clos and the distinctiveness of its terroir in Vincent Dauvissat’s cellar. Vincent proceeded to serve a bottle blind, from 1996. The MW was sure it was Les Clos, and the rhapsodies continued—until Vincent revealed the wine as his Petit Chablis. That was a ‘teachable moment’.

Most abject recent humiliation was calling a crisp-styled Provencal rosé as Marsannay rosé. I don’t drink a lot of the former and tasting in a cellar in Burgundy that seemed the likeliest possibility, but it was an embarrassing error nonetheless.

I enjoy blind tasting the most when I know enough of the parameters (e.g. the producer but not the vineyard or vintage) to know what to look for. That removes the bias of the label without removing the framework. Without a framework of some kind one has to be very disciplined or very lucky.

posts.name: andy velebil
posts.cooked:
edwardmilstein:

btw I try to forget the disasters as quickly as possible

I hear ya on that one! I have far more disasters than successes in blind tastings. At least it can be entertaining when I’m wrong.
Andy- blind tasting is a lot like the quote about golf that I like to relate - which is the golf course humbles all . Anybody that tells you otherwise is probably being disingenuous.

posts.name: Nolan E
posts.cooked:
Ralph Earle:

This is sort of blind: At my last Boston Wine Festival (1990), I was handed a Meritage. I correctly named the percentage of each of the five grapes. At the same event, after tasting a Ferrari-Carano Cabernet Sauvignon I asked the wine maker, “Do you tumble your grapes?” He was astounded, because at that time they did.

The worst stories are within the past five years: 0 for everything on mystery wines my wife brings home from Costco.

What are tumbled grapes?

posts.name: Ad@m Gr0$$m@n
posts.cooked:
One good one - on a group wine expedition a few years ago we were given the challenge to assign varieties to bottles from all the reds in this winemaker’s portfolio of Amador wines. I nailed all five (or was it six), along with two others out of a bus of ~30 people.

Same trip a few years later, different producer, this time Sonoma, slightly fewer parameters. Guess variety and vintage. I had narrowed down each sample to two possibilities and in most cases one of them was correct. Unfortunately I picked the incorrect one most times (e.g. PV instead of PS, well aged restrained Zin vs well aged Cabernet).

Then a friend brought a bottle blind to my place a while back. All I was told was that I had heard of the producer and tasted their wines, otherwise it was a total question. After the initial impression of heat (surprisingly, given the wine), and going around the world a few times, I was fairly convinced it was Italian. Too bad it was a Corison Kronos!

posts.name: Jim Anderson
posts.cooked:
Outside of blending trials in the cellar indo almost no blind tasting. Haven’t belonged to a tasting group for years. Probably the last time was at a post-IPNC Pinot event. So, if I even have good guess stories I don’t remember them.

However, the worst is always when in an ITB blind tasting a winemaker rips a wine and then upon the reveal sees that it is his/her wine begins the huge backpedal on their previous statements. Have seen it many, many times. It’s funny in a vino noir-ish sort of way but is too painful to go through more than once and makes the experience of the tasting painful.

posts.name: Nowell Karten
posts.cooked:
Mark Golodetz:

I was wondering if I should tell this, but on reflection if I don’t mention the name of the winery, and it was thirty years ago, it’s too good a story not to tell. In the late 1980s there used to be a wine weekend held at the Desmond in Albany. One of the highlights was an afternoon session for professionals, where everybody bought a bottle and served it blind. Knowing the owner was going to be there, I brought a bottle of his 1977 California Pinot Noir. The wine served before it had been a Rousseau so it didn’t show well in comparison, in fact was a little sweet and boring. Unfortunately the owner really hated it, saying why bother making wine like this, and ranting for a few minutes as to why wines like this should never be released. Incredibly embarrassing, and a good early lesson in learning what you can and can’t serve blind.



Jim Anderson:

However, the worst is always when in an ITB blind tasting a winemaker rips a wine and then upon the reveal sees that it is his/her wine begins the huge backpedal on their previous statements. Have seen it many, many times. It’s funny in a vino noir-ish sort of way but is too painful to go through more than once and makes the experience of the tasting painful.

I’m glad, then, that the only time I brought an older vintage of a winemaker’s wine to blind-taste him, the wine merely turned out to be corked and, therefore, was immediately unveiled, thereby saving him the above worse embarrassment.
